9(1.412, 0.343) \robotoRobotics:  Science and Systems 2025
Los Angeles, California, June 21-June 25, 2025

Solving Multi-Agent Safe Optimal Control with Distributed Epigraph Form MARL

Songyuan Zhang12, Oswin So12, Mitchell Black3, Zachary Serlin3 and Chuchu Fan2 2Department of Aeronautics and Astronautics, MIT, Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA
Email: {szhang21, oswinso, chuchu}@mit.edu 3MIT Lincoln Laboratory, Lexington, Massachusetts, USA
Email: {mitchell.black, Zachary.Serlin}@ll.mit.edu
Abstract

Tasks for multi-robot systems often require the robots to collaborate and complete a team goal while maintaining safety. This problem is usually formalized as a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP), which targets minimizing a global cost and bringing the mean of constraint violation below a user-defined threshold. Inspired by real-world robotic applications, we define safety as zero constraint violation. While many safe multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) algorithms have been proposed to solve CMDPs, these algorithms suffer from unstable training in this setting. To tackle this, we use the epigraph form for constrained optimization to improve training stability and prove that the centralized epigraph form problem can be solved in a distributed fashion by each agent. This results in a novel centralized training distributed execution MARL algorithm named Def-MARL. Simulation experiments on 8 different tasks across 2 different simulators show that Def-MARL achieves the best overall performance, satisfies safety constraints, and maintains stable training. Real-world hardware experiments on Crazyflie quadcopters demonstrate the ability of Def-MARL to safely coordinate agents to complete complex collaborative tasks compared to other methods.111Project website: https://mit-realm.github.io/def-marl/. 00footnotetext: DISTRIBUTION STATEMENT A. Approved for public release. Distribution is unlimited.

00footnotetext: ∗Equal contribution.

I Introduction

Multi-agent systems (MAS) play an integral role in our aspirations for a more convenient future with examples such as autonomous warehouse operations [37], large-scale autonomous package delivery [45], and traffic routing [76]. These tasks often require designing distributed policies for agents to complete a team goal collaboratively while maintaining safety. To construct distributed policies, multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) under the centralized training distributed execution (CTDE) paradigm [84, 25] has emerged as an attractive method. To incorporate the safety constraints, most MARL algorithms either choose to carefully design their objective function to incorporate soft constraints [69, 58, 79, 74, 54, 57], or model the problem using the constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) [3], which asks for the mean constraint violation to stay below a user-defined threshold [32, 38, 18, 41, 26, 90]. However, real-world robotic applications always require zero constraint violation. While this can be addressed by setting the constraint violation threshold to zero in the CMDP, in this setting the popular Lagrangian methods experience training instabilities which result in sharp drops in performance during training, and non-convergence or convergence to poor policies [66, 35, 24, 36].

These concerns have been identified recently, resulting in a series of works that enforce hard constraints [82, 89, 66, 24, 86] using techniques inspired by Hamilton-Jacobi reachability [71, 44, 49, 46, 5] in deep reinforcement learning (RL) for the single-agent case and have been shown to improve safety compared to other safe RL approaches significantly. However, to the best of our knowledge, theories and algorithms for safe RL are still lacking for the multi-agent scenario, especially when policies are executed in a distributed manner. While single-agent RL methods can be directly applied to the MARL setting by treating the MAS as a centralized single agent, the joint action space grows exponentially with the number of agents, preventing these algorithms from scaling to scenarios with a large number of agents [33, 69, 23].

To tackle the problem of zero constraint violation in multi-agent scenarios with distributed policies222In this paper, the policies are distributed if each agent makes decisions using local information/sensor data and information received via message passing with other agents [25], although this setting is sometimes called “decentralized” in MARL [83]. while achieving high collaborative performance, we propose Distributed epigraph form MARL (Def-MARL) (LABEL:fig:_teaser). Instead of considering the CMDP setting, Def-MARL directly tackles the multi-agent safe optimal control problem (MASOCP), whose solution satisfies zero constraint violation. To solve the MASOCP, Def-MARL uses the epigraph form technique [10], which has previously been shown to yield better policies compared to Lagrangian methods in the single-agent setting [66]. To adapt to the multi-agent setting we consider in this work, we prove that the centralized epigraph form of MASOCP can be solved in a distributed fashion by each agent. Using this result, Def-MARL falls under the CTDE paradigm.

We validate Def-MARL using 8 different tasks from 2 different simulators, multi-particle environments (MPE) [40] and Safe Multi-agent MuJoCo [32], with varying numbers of agents, and compare its performance with existing safe MARL algorithms using the penalty and Lagrangian methods. The results suggest that Def-MARL achieves the best performance while satisfying safety: it is as safe as conservative baselines that achieve high safety but sacrifice performance, while matching the performance of unsafe baselines that sacrifice safety for high performance. In addition, while the baseline methods require different choices of hyperparameters to perform well in different environments and suffer from unstable training because of zero constraint violation threshold, Def-MARL is stable in training using the same hyperparameters across all environments, indicative of the algorithm’s robustness to environmental changes.

We also perform real-world hardware experiments using the Crazyflie (CF) drones [27] on two complex collaborative tasks and compare Def-MARL with both centralized and decentralized model predictive control (MPC) methods [29]. The results indicate that Def-MARL finishes the tasks with 100%percent100100\%100 % safety rates and success rates, while the MPC methods get stuck in local minima or have unsafe behaviors.

To summarize, our contributions are presented below:

  • •

    Drawing on prior work that addresses the training instability of Lagrangian methods in the zero-constraint violation setting, we extend the epigraph form method from single-agent RL to MARL, improving upon the training instability of existing MARL algorithms.

  • •

    We present theoretical results showing that the outer problem of the epigraph form can be decomposed and solved in a distributed manner during online execution. This allows Def-MARL to fall under the CTDE paradigm.

  • •

    We illustrate through extensive simulations that, without any hyperparameter tuning, Def-MARL achieves stable training and is as safe as the most conservative baseline while simultaneously being as performant as the most aggressive baseline across all environments.

  • •

    We demonstrate on Crazyflie drones in hardware that Def-MARL can safely coordinate agents to complete complex collaborative tasks. Def-MARL performs the task better than centralized/decentralized MPC methods and does not get stuck in suboptimal local minima or exhibit unsafe behaviors.

II Related work

Unconstrained MARL. Early works that approach the problem of safety for MARL focus on navigation problems and collision avoidance [14, 13, 21, 64], where safety is achieved by a sparse collision penalty [39], or a shaped reward penalizing getting close to obstacles and neighboring agents [14, 13, 21, 64]. However, adding a penalty to the reward function changes the original objective function, so the resulting policy may not be optimal for the original constraint optimization problem. In addition, the satisfaction of collision avoidance constraints is not necessarily guaranteed by even the optimal policy [47, 21, 39].

Shielding for Safe MARL. One popular method that provides safety to learning-based methods is using shielding or a safety filter [25]. Here, an unconstrained learning method is paired with a shield or safety filter using techniques such as predictive safety filters [88, 50], control barrier functions [11, 55], or automata [19, 77, 48, 7]. Such shields are often constructed before the learning begins and are used to modify either the feasible actions or the output of the learned policy to maintain safety. One benefit is that safety can be guaranteed during both training and deployment since the shield is constructed before training. However, they require domain expertise to build a valid shield, which can be challenging in the single-agent setting and even more difficult for MAS [25]. Other methods can automatically synthesize shields but face scalability challenges [48, 20]. Another drawback is that the policy after shielding might not consider the same objective as the original policy and may result in noncollaborative behaviors or deadlocks [56, 85, 87].

Constrained MARL. In contrast to unconstrained MARL methods, which change the constraint optimization problem to an unconstrained problem, constrained MARL methods explicitly solve the CMDP problem. For the single-agent case, prominent methods for solving CMDPs include primal methods [78], primal-dual methods using Lagrange multipliers [8, 70, 35, 36], and trust-region-based approaches [1, 35]. These methods provide guarantees either in the form of asymptotic convergence guarantees to the optimal (safe) solution [8, 70] using stochastic approximation theory [59, 9], or recursive feasibility of intermediate policies [1, 60] using ideas from trust region optimization [61]. The survey [31] provides an overview of the different methods of solving safety-constrained single-agent RL. In multi-agent cases, however, the problem becomes more difficult because of the non-stationary behavior of other agents, and similar approaches have been presented only recently [32, 38, 18, 41, 26, 90, 15]. However, the CMDP setting they handle makes it difficult for them to handle hard constraints, and results in poor performance with zero constraint violation threshold [24].

Model predictive control. Distributed MPC methods have been proposed to handle MAS, incorporating multi-agent path planning, machine learning, and distributed optimization [75, 72, 92, 22, 42, 16, 52]. However, the solution quality of nonlinear optimizers used to solve MPC when the objective function and constraints are nonlinear highly depends on the initial guess [73, 29]. Moreover, the real-time nonlinear optimizers typically require access to (accurate) first and second-order derivatives [53, 29], which present challenges when trying to solve tasks that have non-differentiable or discontinuous cost functions and constraints such as the ones we consider in this work.

III Problem setting and preliminaries

III-A Multi-agent safe optimal control problem

We consider the multi-agent safe optimal control problem (MASOCP) as defined below. Consider a homogeneous MAS with N𝑁Nitalic_N agents. At time step k𝑘kitalic_k, the global state and control input are given by xk∈𝒳⊆ℝnsuperscript𝑥𝑘𝒳superscriptℝ𝑛x^{k}\in\mathcal{X}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∈ caligraphic_X ⊆ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_n end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and uk∈𝒰⊆ℝmsuperscript𝑢𝑘𝒰superscriptℝ𝑚u^{k}\in\mathcal{U}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{m}italic_u start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∈ caligraphic_U ⊆ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_m end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, respectively. The global control vector is defined by concatenation uk≔[u1k;…;uNk]≔superscript𝑢𝑘superscriptsubscript𝑢1𝑘…superscriptsubscript𝑢𝑁𝑘u^{k}\coloneqq[u_{1}^{k};\dots;u_{N}^{k}]italic_u start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ≔ [ italic_u start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; … ; italic_u start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ], where uik∈𝒰isuperscriptsubscript𝑢𝑖𝑘subscript𝒰𝑖u_{i}^{k}\in\mathcal{U}_{i}italic_u start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∈ caligraphic_U start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is the control input of agent i𝑖iitalic_i. We consider the general nonlinear discrete-time dynamics for the MAS:

xk+1=f⁢(xk,uk),superscript𝑥𝑘1𝑓superscript𝑥𝑘superscript𝑢𝑘x^{k+1}=f(x^{k},u^{k}),italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_f ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_u start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , (1)

where f:𝒳×𝒰→𝒳:𝑓→𝒳𝒰𝒳f:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{U}\to\mathcal{X}italic_f : caligraphic_X × caligraphic_U → caligraphic_X is the global dynamics function. We consider the partially observable setting, where each agent has a limited communication radius R>0𝑅0R>0italic_R > 0 and can only communicate with other agents or observe the environment within its communication region. Denote oik=Oi⁢(xk)∈𝒪⊆ℝnosuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑘subscript𝑂𝑖superscript𝑥𝑘𝒪superscriptℝsubscript𝑛𝑜o_{i}^{k}=O_{i}(x^{k})\in\mathcal{O}\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{n_{o}}italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ∈ caligraphic_O ⊆ blackboard_R start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_n start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_o end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT as the vector of the information observed by agent i𝑖iitalic_i at the time step k𝑘kitalic_k, where Oi:𝒳→𝒪:subscript𝑂𝑖→𝒳𝒪O_{i}:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{O}italic_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT : caligraphic_X → caligraphic_O is an encoding function of the information shared from neighbors of agent i𝑖iitalic_i and the observed data of the environment. We allow multi-hop communication between agents, so an agent may communicate with another agent outside its communication region if a communication path exists between them.

Let the avoid/unsafe set of agent i𝑖iitalic_i be 𝒜i≔{oi∈𝒪:hi⁢(oi)>0}≔subscript𝒜𝑖conditional-setsubscript𝑜𝑖𝒪subscriptℎ𝑖subscript𝑜𝑖0\mathcal{A}_{i}\coloneqq\{o_{i}\in\mathcal{O}:h_{i}(o_{i})>0\}caligraphic_A start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≔ { italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∈ caligraphic_O : italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) > 0 }, for some function hi:𝒪→ℝ:subscriptℎ𝑖→𝒪ℝh_{i}:\mathcal{O}\to\mathbb{R}italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT : caligraphic_O → blackboard_R. The global avoid set is then defined as 𝒜≔{x∈𝒳:h⁢(x)>0}≔𝒜conditional-set𝑥𝒳ℎ𝑥0\mathcal{A}\coloneqq\{x\in\mathcal{X}:h(x)>0\}caligraphic_A ≔ { italic_x ∈ caligraphic_X : italic_h ( italic_x ) > 0 }, where h⁢(x)=maxi⁡hi⁢(oi)=maxi⁡hi⁢(Oi⁢(x))ℎ𝑥subscript𝑖subscriptℎ𝑖subscript𝑜𝑖subscript𝑖subscriptℎ𝑖subscript𝑂𝑖𝑥h(x)=\max_{i}h_{i}(o_{i})=\max_{i}h_{i}(O_{i}(x))italic_h ( italic_x ) = roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) = roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ). In other words, ∃i,s.t.oi∈𝒜i⇔x∈𝒜\exists i,\;\mathrm{s.t.}\;o_{i}\in\mathcal{A}_{i}\iff x\in\mathcal{A}∃ italic_i , roman_s . roman_t . italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∈ caligraphic_A start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ⇔ italic_x ∈ caligraphic_A. Given a global cost function l:𝒳×𝒰→ℝ:𝑙→𝒳𝒰ℝl:\mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{U}\to\mathbb{R}italic_l : caligraphic_X × caligraphic_U → blackboard_R describing the task for the agents to accomplish333The cost function l𝑙litalic_l is not the cost in CMDP. Rather, it corresponds to the negation of the reward in CMDP., we aim to find distributed control policies πi:𝒪→𝒰i:subscript𝜋𝑖→𝒪subscript𝒰𝑖\pi_{i}:\mathcal{O}\to\mathcal{U}_{i}italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT : caligraphic_O → caligraphic_U start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT such that starting from any given initial states x0∉𝒜superscript𝑥0𝒜x^{0}\notin\mathcal{A}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∉ caligraphic_A, the policies keep the agents outside the avoid set 𝒜𝒜\mathcal{A}caligraphic_A and minimize the infinite horizon cost. In other words, denoting π:𝒳→𝒰:𝜋→𝒳𝒰\pi:\mathcal{X}\to\mathcal{U}italic_π : caligraphic_X → caligraphic_U as the joint policy such that π⁢(x)=[π1⁢(o1);…;πN⁢(oN)]=[π1⁢(O1⁢(x));…;πN⁢(ON⁢(x))]𝜋𝑥subscript𝜋1subscript𝑜1…subscript𝜋𝑁subscript𝑜𝑁subscript𝜋1subscript𝑂1𝑥…subscript𝜋𝑁subscript𝑂𝑁𝑥\pi(x)=[\pi_{1}(o_{1});\dots;\pi_{N}(o_{N})]=[\pi_{1}(O_{1}(x));\dots;\pi_{N}(% O_{N}(x))]italic_π ( italic_x ) = [ italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ; … ; italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ] = [ italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ) ; … ; italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x ) ) ], we aim to solve the following infinite-horizon MASOCP for a given initial state x0superscript𝑥0x^{0}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT:

min{πi}i=1Nsubscriptsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝜋𝑖𝑖1𝑁\displaystyle\min_{\{\pi_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}}\quadroman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT { italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT } start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∑k=0∞l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))superscriptsubscript𝑘0𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∞ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) (2a)
s.t.formulae-sequencest\displaystyle\mathrm{s.t.}\quadroman_s . roman_t . hi⁢(Oi⁢(xk))≤0,subscriptℎ𝑖subscript𝑂𝑖superscript𝑥𝑘0\displaystyle h_{i}(O_{i}(x^{k}))\leq 0,italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_O start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) ≤ 0 , ∀i∈{1,…,N},k≥0,formulae-sequencefor-all𝑖1…𝑁𝑘0\displaystyle\forall i\in\{1,\dots,N\},k\geq 0,∀ italic_i ∈ { 1 , … , italic_N } , italic_k ≥ 0 , (2b)
xk+1=f⁢(xk,π⁢(xk)),superscript𝑥𝑘1𝑓superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘\displaystyle x^{k+1}=f(x^{k},\pi(x^{k})),italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_f ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) , k≥0.𝑘0\displaystyle k\geq 0.italic_k ≥ 0 . (2c)

Note that the safety constraint (2b) differs from the average constraints considered in CMDPs [3]. Consequently, instead of allowing safety violations to occur as long as the mean constraint violation is below a threshold, this formulation disallows any constraint violation. From hereon, we omit the dynamics constraint (2c) for conciseness.

III-B Epigraph form

Existing methods are unable to solve (2) well. This has been observed previously in the single-agent setting [82, 89, 66, 24]. We show later that the poor performance of methods that tackle the CMDP setting to the constrained problem (2) also translates to the multi-agent setting, as we observe a similar phenomenon in our experiments (Section V). Namely, although unconstrained MARL can be used to solve (2) using the penalty method [51], this does not perform well in practice, where a small penalty results in policies that violate constraints, and a large penalty results in higher total costs. The Lagrangian method [32] can solve the problem theoretically, but it suffers from unstable training and has poor performance in practice when the constraint violation threshold is zero [66, 24]. In this section, we introduce a new method of solving (2) that can mitigate the above problems by extending prior work [66] to the multi-agent setting.

Given a constrained optimization problem with objective function J𝐽Jitalic_J (e.g., J=∑k=0∞l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))𝐽superscriptsubscript𝑘0𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘J=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))italic_J = ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∞ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) as in (2a)), and constraints hℎhitalic_h (e.g., (2b)):

minπJ(π)s.t.h(π)≤0,\displaystyle\min_{{\pi}}\quad J({\pi})\qquad\mathrm{s.t.}\quad h({\pi})\leq 0,roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_π end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_J ( italic_π ) roman_s . roman_t . italic_h ( italic_π ) ≤ 0 , (3)

its epigraph form [10] is given as

minπ,zzs.t.h(π)≤0,J(π)≤z,\displaystyle\min_{{\pi},z}\quad z\qquad\mathrm{s.t.}\quad h({\pi})\leq 0,% \quad J({\pi})\leq z,roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_π , italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z roman_s . roman_t . italic_h ( italic_π ) ≤ 0 , italic_J ( italic_π ) ≤ italic_z , (4)

where z∈ℝ𝑧ℝz\in\mathbb{R}italic_z ∈ blackboard_R is an auxiliary variable. In other words, we add a constraint to enforce z𝑧zitalic_z as an upper bound of the cost J⁢(π)𝐽𝜋J(\pi)italic_J ( italic_π ), then minimize z𝑧zitalic_z. The solution to (4) is identical to the original problem (3) [10]. Furthermore, (4) is equivalent [66] to

minzsubscript𝑧\displaystyle\min_{z}roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT z𝑧\displaystyle\quad zitalic_z (5a)
s.t.formulae-sequencest\displaystyle\mathrm{s.t.}roman_s . roman_t . minπ⁡Jz⁢(π,z)≔max⁡{h⁢(π),J⁢(π)−z}≤0≔subscript𝜋subscript𝐽𝑧𝜋𝑧ℎ𝜋𝐽𝜋𝑧0\displaystyle\quad\min_{{\pi}}J_{z}({\pi},z)\coloneqq\max\{h({\pi}),J({\pi})-z% \}\leq 0roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_π end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_J start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_π , italic_z ) ≔ roman_max { italic_h ( italic_π ) , italic_J ( italic_π ) - italic_z } ≤ 0 (5b)

As a result, the original constrained problem (3) is decomposed into the following two subproblems:

  1. 1.

    An unconstrained inner problem (5b), where, given an arbitrary desired cost upper bound z𝑧zitalic_z, we find π𝜋{\pi}italic_π such that Jz⁢(π,z)subscript𝐽𝑧𝜋𝑧J_{z}({\pi},z)italic_J start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_π , italic_z ) is minimized, i.e., best satisfies the constraints h≤0ℎ0h\leq 0italic_h ≤ 0 and J≤z𝐽𝑧J\leq zitalic_J ≤ italic_z.

  2. 2.

    A 1111-dimensional constrained outer problem (5a) over z𝑧zitalic_z, which finds the smallest cost upper bound z𝑧zitalic_z such that z𝑧zitalic_z is indeed a cost upper bound (J≤z)J\leq z)italic_J ≤ italic_z ) and the constraints of the original problem h⁢(π)≤0ℎ𝜋0h({\pi})\leq 0italic_h ( italic_π ) ≤ 0 holds.

Comparison with the Lagrangian method. Another popular way to solve MASOCP (2) is the Lagrangian method [32]. However, it suffers from unstable training when considering the zero constraint violation [66, 35] setting. More specifically, this refers to the case with constraints ∑k=0∞c⁢(xk)≤0superscriptsubscript𝑘0𝑐superscript𝑥𝑘0\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}c(x^{k})\leq 0∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∞ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_c ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ≤ 0 for c:𝒳→ℝ≥0:𝑐→𝒳subscriptℝabsent0c:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}italic_c : caligraphic_X → blackboard_R start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≥ 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT non-negative. Since hℎhitalic_h can be negative, we can convert our problem setting (3) to the zero constraint violation setting by taking c⁢(x)≔max⁡{0,h⁢(x)}≔𝑐𝑥0ℎ𝑥c(x)\coloneqq\max\{0,h(x)\}italic_c ( italic_x ) ≔ roman_max { 0 , italic_h ( italic_x ) }. Then, (3) reads as

minπJ(π)s.t.∑k=0∞max{0,h(xk)}≤0.\min_{\pi}\quad J(\pi)\qquad\mathrm{s.t.}\quad\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\max\{0,h(x^{% k})\}\leq 0.roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_π end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_J ( italic_π ) roman_s . roman_t . ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∞ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_max { 0 , italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) } ≤ 0 . (6)

The Lagrangian form of (6) is then

maxλ≥0⁡minπJλ⁢(π,λ)≔J⁢(π)+λ⁢∑k=0∞max⁡{h⁢(xk),0},≔subscript𝜆0subscript𝜋subscript𝐽𝜆𝜋𝜆𝐽𝜋𝜆superscriptsubscript𝑘0ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘0\max_{\lambda\geq 0}\min_{\pi}\quad J_{\lambda}(\pi,\lambda)\coloneqq J(\pi)+% \lambda\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\max\{h(x^{k}),0\},roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_λ ≥ 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_π end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_J start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_λ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_π , italic_λ ) ≔ italic_J ( italic_π ) + italic_λ ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∞ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , 0 } , (7)

where λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ is the Lagrangian multiplier and is updated with gradient ascent. However, ∂∂λ⁢Jλ⁢(π,λ)=∑k=0∞max⁡{h⁢(xk),0}≥0𝜆subscript𝐽𝜆𝜋𝜆superscriptsubscript𝑘0ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘00\frac{\partial}{\partial\lambda}J_{\lambda}({\pi},\lambda)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}% \max\{h(x^{k}),0\}\geq 0divide start_ARG ∂ end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_λ end_ARG italic_J start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_λ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_π , italic_λ ) = ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ∞ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , 0 } ≥ 0, so λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ continuously increases and never decreases. As ∂∂π⁢Jλ⁢(π,λ)𝜋subscript𝐽𝜆𝜋𝜆\frac{\partial}{\partial{\pi}}J_{\lambda}({\pi},\lambda)divide start_ARG ∂ end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_π end_ARG italic_J start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_λ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_π , italic_λ ) scales linearly in λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ when h⁢(xk)>0ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘0h(x^{k})>0italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) > 0 for some k𝑘kitalic_k, a large value of λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ causes a large gradient w.r.t x𝑥xitalic_x, and makes the training unstable. Note that for the epigraph form, since z𝑧zitalic_z does not multiply with the cost function J𝐽Jitalic_J but is added to J𝐽Jitalic_J in (5b), the gradient ∂∂π⁢Jz⁢(π,z)𝜋subscript𝐽𝑧𝜋𝑧\frac{\partial}{\partial{\pi}}J_{z}({\pi},z)divide start_ARG ∂ end_ARG start_ARG ∂ italic_π end_ARG italic_J start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_π , italic_z ) does not scale with the value of z𝑧zitalic_z resulting in more stable training. We validate this in our experiments (Section V).

IV Distributed epigraph form multi-agent reinforcement learning

In this section, we propose the Distributed epigraph form MARL (Def-MARL) algorithm to solve MASOCP (2) using MARL. First, we transfer MASOCP (2) to its epigraph form with an auxiliary variable z𝑧zitalic_z to model the desired cost upper bound. The epigraph form includes an inner problem and an outer problem. For distributed execution, we provide a theoretical result that the outer problem can be solved distributively by each agent. This allows Def-MARL to fit the CTDE paradigm, where in centralized training, the agents’ policies are trained together given the desired cost upper bound z𝑧zitalic_z, and in distributed execution, the agents distributively find the smallest cost upper bound z𝑧zitalic_z that ensures safety.

IV-A Epigraph form for MASOCP

To rewrite MASOCP (2) into its epigraph form (5), we first define the cost-value function Vlsuperscript𝑉𝑙V^{l}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT for a joint policy π𝜋\piitalic_π using the standard optimal control notation [6]:

Vl⁢(xτ;π)≔∑k≥τl⁢(xk,π⁢(xk)).≔superscript𝑉𝑙superscript𝑥𝜏𝜋subscript𝑘𝜏𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘V^{l}(x^{\tau};\pi)\coloneqq\sum_{k\geq\tau}l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k})).italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) ≔ ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k ≥ italic_τ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) . (8)

We also define the constraint-value function Vhsuperscript𝑉ℎV^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT as the maximum constraint violation:

Vh⁢(xτ;π)superscript𝑉ℎsuperscript𝑥𝜏𝜋\displaystyle V^{h}(x^{\tau};\pi)italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) ≔maxk≥τ⁡h⁢(xk)=maxk≥τ⁡maxi⁡hi⁢(oik)≔absentsubscript𝑘𝜏ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘subscript𝑘𝜏subscript𝑖subscriptℎ𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑘\displaystyle\coloneqq\max_{k\geq\tau}h(x^{k})=\max_{k\geq\tau}\max_{i}h_{i}(o% _{i}^{k})≔ roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k ≥ italic_τ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) = roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k ≥ italic_τ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) (9)
=maxi⁡maxk≥τ⁡hi⁢(oik)=maxi⁡Vih⁢(oiτ;π).absentsubscript𝑖subscript𝑘𝜏subscriptℎ𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑘subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝜏𝜋\displaystyle=\max_{i}\max_{k\geq\tau}h_{i}(o_{i}^{k})=\max_{i}V_{i}^{h}(o_{i}% ^{\tau};\pi).= roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k ≥ italic_τ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) = roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) .

Here, we interchange the max\maxroman_max to define the local per-agent functions Vih⁢(oiτ;π)=maxk≥τ⁡hi⁢(oik)superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝜏𝜋subscript𝑘𝜏subscriptℎ𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝑘V_{i}^{h}(o_{i}^{\tau};\pi)=\max_{k\geq\tau}h_{i}(o_{i}^{k})italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) = roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k ≥ italic_τ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ). Each Vihsuperscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎV_{i}^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT uses only the agent’s local observation and thus is distributed. We now introduce the auxiliary variable z𝑧zitalic_z for the desired upper bound of Vlsuperscript𝑉𝑙V^{l}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, allowing us to restate (2) concisely as

min{πi}i=1NVl(x0;π)s.t.Vh(x0;π)≤0.\displaystyle\min_{\{\pi_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}}\quad V^{l}(x^{0};\pi)\qquad\mathrm{s% .t.}\quad V^{h}(x^{0};\pi)\leq 0.roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT { italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT } start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) roman_s . roman_t . italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) ≤ 0 . (10)

The epigraph form (5) of (10) then takes the form

minzsubscript𝑧\displaystyle\min_{z}roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT z𝑧\displaystyle\quad z\vphantom{\underbrace{\max\big{\{}\big{\}}}_{\coloneqq V}}italic_z (11a)
s.t.formulae-sequencest\displaystyle\mathrm{s.t.}roman_s . roman_t . min{πi}i=1N⁡max⁡{maxi⁡Vih⁢(oiτ;π),Vl⁢(xτ;π)−z}⏟≔V⁢(x0,z;π)≤0.subscriptsuperscriptsubscriptsubscript𝜋𝑖𝑖1𝑁subscript⏟subscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝜏𝜋superscript𝑉𝑙superscript𝑥𝜏𝜋𝑧≔absent𝑉superscript𝑥0𝑧𝜋0\displaystyle\quad\min_{\{\pi_{i}\}_{i=1}^{N}}\,\underbrace{\max\big{\{}\max_{% i}V_{i}^{h}(o_{i}^{\tau};\pi),V^{l}(x^{\tau};\pi)-z\big{\}}}_{\coloneqq V(x^{0% },z;\pi)}\leq 0.roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT { italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT } start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i = 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_N end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT under⏟ start_ARG roman_max { roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) , italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) - italic_z } end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≔ italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ) end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≤ 0 . (11b)

By interpreting the left-hand side of (11b) as a new policy optimization problem, we define the total value function V𝑉Vitalic_V as the objective function to (11b). This can be simplified as

V⁢(xτ,z;π)𝑉superscript𝑥𝜏𝑧𝜋\displaystyle V(x^{\tau},z;\pi)italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ) =max⁡{maxi⁡Vih⁢(oiτ;π),Vl⁢(xτ;π)−z}absentsubscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝜏𝜋superscript𝑉𝑙superscript𝑥𝜏𝜋𝑧\displaystyle=\max\{\max_{i}V_{i}^{h}(o_{i}^{\tau};\pi),V^{l}(x^{\tau};\pi)-z\}= roman_max { roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) , italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) - italic_z } (12)
=maxi⁡max⁡{Vih⁢(oiτ;π),Vl⁢(xτ;π)−z}absentsubscript𝑖superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝜏𝜋superscript𝑉𝑙superscript𝑥𝜏𝜋𝑧\displaystyle=\max_{i}\max\{V_{i}^{h}(o_{i}^{\tau};\pi),V^{l}(x^{\tau};\pi)-z\}= roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max { italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) , italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) - italic_z }
=maxi⁡Vi⁢(xτ,z;π),absentsubscript𝑖subscript𝑉𝑖superscript𝑥𝜏𝑧𝜋\displaystyle=\max_{i}V_{i}(x^{\tau},z;\pi),= roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ) ,

Again, we interchange the max\maxroman_max to define Vi⁢(xτ,z;π)=max⁡{Vih⁢(oiτ;π),Vl⁢(xτ;π)−z}subscript𝑉𝑖superscript𝑥𝜏𝑧𝜋superscriptsubscript𝑉𝑖ℎsuperscriptsubscript𝑜𝑖𝜏𝜋superscript𝑉𝑙superscript𝑥𝜏𝜋𝑧V_{i}(x^{\tau},z;\pi)=\max\{V_{i}^{h}(o_{i}^{\tau};\pi),V^{l}(x^{\tau};\pi)-z\}italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ) = roman_max { italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) , italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_τ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) - italic_z } as the per-agent total value function. Using this to rewrite (11) then yields

minzsubscript𝑧\displaystyle\min_{z}roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z end_POSTSUBSCRIPT z𝑧\displaystyle\quad z\vphantom{\Big{(}}italic_z (13a)
s.t.formulae-sequencest\displaystyle\mathrm{s.t.}roman_s . roman_t . minπ⁡maxi⁡Vi⁢(x0,z;π)≤0.subscript𝜋subscript𝑖subscript𝑉𝑖superscript𝑥0𝑧𝜋0\displaystyle\quad{\min_{\pi}}\max_{i}V_{i}(x^{0},z;\pi)\leq 0.\vphantom{\Big{% (}}roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_π end_POSTSUBSCRIPT roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ) ≤ 0 . (13b)

This decomposes the original problem (2) into an unconstrained inner problem (13b) over policy π𝜋\piitalic_π and a constrained outer problem (13a) over z𝑧zitalic_z. During offline training, we solve the inner problem (13b): for parameter z𝑧zitalic_z, find the optimal policy π⁢(⋅,z)𝜋⋅𝑧\pi(\cdot,z)italic_π ( ⋅ , italic_z ) to minimize V⁢(x0,z;π)𝑉superscript𝑥0𝑧𝜋V(x^{0},z;\pi)italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ). Note that the optimal policy of the inner problem depends on z𝑧zitalic_z. During execution, we solve the outer problem (13a) online to get the minimal z𝑧zitalic_z that satisfies constraint (13b). Using this z𝑧zitalic_z in the z𝑧zitalic_z-conditioned policy π⁢(⋅,z)𝜋⋅𝑧\pi(\cdot,z)italic_π ( ⋅ , italic_z ) found in the inner problem gives us the optimal policy for the overall epigraph form MASOCP (EF-MASOCP).

To solve the inner problem (13b), the total value function V𝑉Vitalic_V must be amenable to dynamic programming, which we show in the following proposition.

Proposition 1

Dynamic programming can be applied to EF-MASOCP (13), resulting in

V⁢(xk,zk;π)𝑉superscript𝑥𝑘superscript𝑧𝑘𝜋\displaystyle V(x^{k},z^{k};\pi)italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) =max⁡{h⁢(xk),V⁢(xk+1,zk+1;π)},absentℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘𝑉superscript𝑥𝑘1superscript𝑧𝑘1𝜋\displaystyle=\max\{h(x^{k}),V(x^{k+1},z^{k+1};\pi)\},= roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) } , (14)
zk+1superscript𝑧𝑘1\displaystyle\quad z^{k+1}italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT =zk−l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk)).absentsuperscript𝑧𝑘𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘\displaystyle=z^{k}-l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k})).= italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) .

The proof of 1 is provided in Appendix A following the proof of the single-agent version [66]. In other words, for a given cost upper bound zksuperscript𝑧𝑘z^{k}italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, the value function V𝑉Vitalic_V at the current state xksuperscript𝑥𝑘x^{k}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT can be computed using the value function at the next state xk+1superscript𝑥𝑘1x^{k+1}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT but with a different cost upper bound zk+1=zk−l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))superscript𝑧𝑘1superscript𝑧𝑘𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘z^{k+1}=z^{k}-l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) which itself is a function of zksuperscript𝑧𝑘z^{k}italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. This can be interpreted as a “dynamics” for the cost upper bound z𝑧zitalic_z. Intuitively, if we wish to satisfy the upper bound zksuperscript𝑧𝑘z^{k}italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT but suffer a cost l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ), then the upper bound at the next time step should be smaller by l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) so that the total cost from xksuperscript𝑥𝑘x^{k}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT remains upper bounded by zksuperscript𝑧𝑘z^{k}italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. Additional discussion on 1 is provided in Appendix C.

Remark 1 (Effect of z𝑧zitalic_z on the learned policy)

From (12), for a fixed x𝑥xitalic_x and π𝜋\piitalic_π, observe that for z𝑧zitalic_z large enough (i.e., Vl⁢(x;π)−zsuperscript𝑉𝑙𝑥𝜋𝑧{V^{l}(x;\pi)}-zitalic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ; italic_π ) - italic_z is small enough), we have V⁢(x,z;π)=Vh⁢(x;π)𝑉𝑥𝑧𝜋superscript𝑉ℎ𝑥𝜋V(x,z;\pi)=V^{h}(x;\pi)italic_V ( italic_x , italic_z ; italic_π ) = italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ; italic_π ). Consequently, taking a gradient step on V⁢(x,z;π)𝑉𝑥𝑧𝜋V(x,z;\pi)italic_V ( italic_x , italic_z ; italic_π ) equals taking a gradient step on Vh⁢(x;π)superscript𝑉ℎ𝑥𝜋V^{h}(x;\pi)italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ; italic_π ), which reduces the constraint violation. Otherwise, V⁢(x,z;π)=Vl⁢(x;π)−z𝑉𝑥𝑧𝜋superscript𝑉𝑙𝑥𝜋𝑧V(x,z;\pi)=V^{l}(x;\pi)-zitalic_V ( italic_x , italic_z ; italic_π ) = italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ; italic_π ) - italic_z. Taking gradient steps on V⁢(x,z;π)𝑉𝑥𝑧𝜋V(x,z;\pi)italic_V ( italic_x , italic_z ; italic_π ) equals taking gradient steps on Vl⁢(x;π)superscript𝑉𝑙𝑥𝜋V^{l}(x;\pi)italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x ; italic_π ), which reduces the total cost.

IV-B Solving the inner problem using MARL

Following So and Fan [66], we solve the inner problem using centralized training with proximal policy optimization (PPO) [63]. We use a graph neural network (GNN) backbone for the z𝑧zitalic_z-conditioned policy πθ⁢(oi,z)subscript𝜋𝜃subscript𝑜𝑖𝑧\pi_{\theta}(o_{i},z)italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_θ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z ), cost-value function Vϕl⁢(x,z)subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑙italic-ϕ𝑥𝑧V^{l}_{\phi}(x,z)italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ϕ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x , italic_z ), and the constraint-value function Vψh⁢(oi,z)subscriptsuperscript𝑉ℎ𝜓subscript𝑜𝑖𝑧V^{h}_{\psi}(o_{i},z)italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ψ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z ) with parameters θ𝜃\thetaitalic_θ, ϕitalic-ϕ\phiitalic_ϕ, and ψ𝜓\psiitalic_ψ, respectively. Note that other neural network (NN) structures can be used as well. The implementation details are introduced in Appendix E.

Policy and value function updates. During centralized training, the NNs are trained to solve the inner problem (13b), i.e., for a randomly sampled z𝑧zitalic_z, find policy π⁢(⋅,z)𝜋⋅𝑧\pi(\cdot,z)italic_π ( ⋅ , italic_z ) that minimizes the total value function V⁢(x0,z;π)𝑉superscript𝑥0𝑧𝜋V(x^{0},z;\pi)italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z ; italic_π ). We follow MAPPO [80] to train the NNs. Specifically, when calculating the advantage with the generated advantage estimation (GAE) [62] for the i𝑖iitalic_i-th agent, Aisubscript𝐴𝑖A_{i}italic_A start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT [63], instead of using the cost function Vlsuperscript𝑉𝑙V^{l}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT [80], we apply the decomposed total value function max⁡{Vψh⁢(oi,z),Vϕl⁢(x,z)−z}subscriptsuperscript𝑉ℎ𝜓subscript𝑜𝑖𝑧subscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑙italic-ϕ𝑥𝑧𝑧\max\{V^{h}_{\psi}(o_{i},z),V^{l}_{\phi}(x,z)-z\}roman_max { italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ψ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z ) , italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ϕ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_x , italic_z ) - italic_z }. We perform trajectory rollouts following the dynamics for x𝑥xitalic_x (1) and z𝑧zitalic_z (14) using the learned policy πθsubscript𝜋𝜃\pi_{\theta}italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_θ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, starting from random sampled x0superscript𝑥0x^{0}italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT and z0superscript𝑧0z^{0}italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. After collecting the trajectories, we train the cost-value function Vϕlsubscriptsuperscript𝑉𝑙italic-ϕV^{l}_{\phi}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ϕ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and the constraint-value function Vψhsubscriptsuperscript𝑉ℎ𝜓V^{h}_{\psi}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ψ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT via regression and use the PPO policy loss to update the z𝑧zitalic_z-conditioned policy πθsubscript𝜋𝜃\pi_{\theta}italic_π start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_θ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT.

IV-C Solving the outer problem during distributed execution

During execution, we solve the outer problem of EF-MASOCP (13) online. However, the outer problem is still centralized because the constraint (13b) requires the centralized cost-value function Vlsuperscript𝑉𝑙V^{l}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. To achieve a distributed policy during execution, we introduce the following theoretical result:

Theorem 1

Assume no two unique values of z𝑧zitalic_z achieves the same unique cost. Then, the outer problem of EF-MASOCP (5a) is equivalent to the following:

z𝑧\displaystyle zitalic_z =maxi⁡ziabsentsubscript𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖\displaystyle=\max_{i}z_{i}= roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT (15a)
zi=minz′z′s.t.Vih(oi;π(⋅,z′))≤0,i=1,⋯,N.\displaystyle\begin{split}z_{i}&=\min_{z^{\prime}}\quad z^{\prime}\\ &\hskip 16.00008pt\mathrm{s.t.}\quad V_{i}^{h}(o_{i};\pi(\cdot,z^{\prime}))% \leq 0,\quad i=1,\cdots,N.\end{split}start_ROW start_CELL italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT end_CELL start_CELL = roman_min start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ′ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ′ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_CELL end_ROW start_ROW start_CELL end_CELL start_CELL roman_s . roman_t . italic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ; italic_π ( ⋅ , italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ′ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) ≤ 0 , italic_i = 1 , ⋯ , italic_N . end_CELL end_ROW (15b)

The proof is provided in Appendix B. 1 enables computing z𝑧zitalic_z without the use of the centralized Vlsuperscript𝑉𝑙V^{l}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT during execution. Specifically, each agent i𝑖iitalic_i solves the local problem (15b) for zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, which is a 1-dimensional optimization problem and can be efficiently solved using root-finding methods (e.g., [12]) as in [66], then communicates zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT among the other agents to obtain the maximum (15a). One challenge is that this maximum may not be computable if the agents are not connected. However, in our problem setting, if one agent is not connected, it does not appear in the observations o𝑜oitalic_o of other connected agents. Therefore, it would not contribute to the constraint-value function Vhsuperscript𝑉ℎV^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT of other agents. As a result, it is sufficient for only the connected agents to communicate their zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. Furthermore, we observe experimentally that the agents can achieve low cost while maintaining safety even if zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT is not communicated (see Section V-C). Thus, we do not include zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT communication for our method. The overall framework of Def-MARL is provided in Fig. 1.

Dealing with estimation errors. Since there may be errors estimating Vhsuperscript𝑉ℎV^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT using NN, we can reduce the resulting safety violation by modifying hℎhitalic_h to add a buffer region. Specifically, for a constant ν>0𝜈0\nu>0italic_ν > 0, we modify hℎhitalic_h such that h≥νℎ𝜈h\geq\nuitalic_h ≥ italic_ν when the constraints are violated and h≤−νℎ𝜈h\leq-\nuitalic_h ≤ - italic_ν otherwise. We then modify (15b) to Vψh⁢(oi,zi)≤−ξsubscriptsuperscript𝑉ℎ𝜓subscript𝑜𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖𝜉V^{h}_{\psi}(o_{i},z_{i})\leq-\xiitalic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ψ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ≤ - italic_ξ, where ξ∈[0,ν]𝜉0𝜈\xi\in[0,\nu]italic_ξ ∈ [ 0 , italic_ν ] is a hyperparameter (where we want ξ≈ν𝜉𝜈\xi\approx\nuitalic_ξ ≈ italic_ν to emphasize more on safety). This makes z𝑧zitalic_z more robust to estimation errors of Vhsuperscript𝑉ℎV^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. We study the importance of ξ𝜉\xiitalic_ξ in Section V-C.

V Simulation experiments

In this section, we design simulation experiments to answer the following research questions:

  1. (Q1):

    Does Def-MARL satisfy the safety constraints and achieve low cost with constant hyperparameters across all environments?

  2. (Q2):

    Can Def-MARL achieve the global optimum of the original constrained optimization problem?

  3. (Q3):

    How stable is the training of Def-MARL?

  4. (Q4):

    How well does Def-MARL scale to larger MAS?

  5. (Q5):

    Does the learned policy from Def-MARL generalize to larger MAS?

Details for the implementation, environments, and hyperparameters are provided in Appendix E.

V-A Setup

Environments. We evaluate Def-MARL in two sets of simulation environments: modified Multi-agent Particle Environments (MPE) [40], and Safe Multi-agent MuJoCo environments [32] (see Fig. 2). In MPE, the agents are assumed to have double integrator dynamics with bounded continuous action spaces [−1,1]2superscript112[-1,1]^{2}[ - 1 , 1 ] start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT 2 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT. We provide the full details of all tasks in Appendix E. To increase the difficulty of the tasks, we add 3333 static obstacles to these environments. For Safe Multi-agent MuJoCo environments, we consider Safe HalfCheetah 2x3 and Safe Coupled HalfCheetah 4x3. The agents must collaborate to make the cheetah run as fast as possible without colliding with a moving wall in front. To design the constraint function hℎhitalic_h, we let ν=0.5𝜈0.5\nu=0.5italic_ν = 0.5 in all our experiments and ξ=0.4𝜉0.4\xi=0.4italic_ξ = 0.4 when solving the outer problem.

Baselines. We compare our algorithm with the state-of-the-art (SOTA) MARL algorithm InforMARL [51] with a constraint-penalized cost l′⁢(x,u)=l⁢(x,u)+β⁢max⁡{h⁢(x),0}superscript𝑙′𝑥𝑢𝑙𝑥𝑢𝛽ℎ𝑥0l^{\prime}(x,u)=l(x,u)+\beta\max\{h(x),0\}italic_l start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ′ end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_x , italic_u ) = italic_l ( italic_x , italic_u ) + italic_β roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x ) , 0 }, where β∈{0.02,0.1,0.5}𝛽0.020.10.5\beta\in\{0.02,0.1,0.5\}italic_β ∈ { 0.02 , 0.1 , 0.5 } is a penalty parameter, and denote this baseline as Penalty(β𝛽\betaitalic_β). We also consider the SOTA safe MARL algorithm MAPPO-Lagrangian [30, 32]444We omit the comparison with MACPO [30, 32] as it was shown to perform similarly to MAPPO-Lagrangian but have significantly worse time complexity and wall clock time for training.. In addition, because the learning rate of the Lagrangian multiplier λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ is tiny (10−7superscript10710^{-7}10 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 7 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT) in the official implementation of MAPPO-Lagrangian [32], the value of λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ during training will be largely determined by the initial value λ0subscript𝜆0\lambda_{0}italic_λ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT of λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ. We thus consider two λ0∈{1,5}subscript𝜆015\lambda_{0}\in\{1,5\}italic_λ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ∈ { 1 , 5 }. Moreover, to compare the training stability, we consider increasing the learning rate of λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ in MAPPO-Lagrangian to 3×10−33superscript1033\times 10^{-3}3 × 10 start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - 3 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT.555This is the smallest learning rate for λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ that does not make MAPPO-Lagrangian ignore the safety constraint. We set λ0=0.78subscript𝜆00.78\lambda_{0}=0.78italic_λ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 0.78 following [32]. For a fair comparison, we reimplement MAPPO-Lagrangian using the same GNN backbone as used in Def-MARL and InforMARL, denoted as Lagr(λ0subscript𝜆0\lambda_{0}italic_λ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT) and Lagr(lr) for the increased learning rate one. We run each method for the same number of update steps, which is large enough for all the methods to converge.

Evaluation criteria. Following the objective of MASOCP, we use the cost and safety rate as the evaluation criteria for the performance of all algorithms. The cost is the cumulative cost over the trajectory ∑k=0Tl⁢(xk,uk)superscriptsubscript𝑘0𝑇𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘superscript𝑢𝑘\sum_{k=0}^{T}l(x^{k},u^{k})∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_k = 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_T end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_u start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ). The safety rate is defined as the ratio of agents that remain safe over the entire trajectory, i.e., hi⁢(oik)≤0,∀ksubscriptℎ𝑖subscriptsuperscript𝑜𝑘𝑖0for-all𝑘h_{i}(o^{k}_{i})\leq 0,\forall kitalic_h start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ≤ 0 , ∀ italic_k, over all agents. Unlike the CMDP setting, we do not report the mean of constraint violations over time but the violation of the hard safety constraints.

V-B Results

We train all algorithms with 3333 different random seeds and test the converged policies on 32323232 different initial conditions. As discussed in Section IV-C, we disable the communication of zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT between agents (investigated in Section V-C). We draw the following conclusions.

(Q1): Def-MARL achieves the best performance with constant hyperparameters across all environments. First, we plot the safety rate (y-axis) and cumulative cost (x-axis) for each algorithm in Fig. 3. Thus, the closer an algorithm is to the top-left corner, the better it performs. In both MPE and Safe Multi-agent MuJoCo environments, Def-MARL is always closest to the top-left corner, maintaining a low cost while having near 100%percent100100\%100 % safety rate. For the baselines Penalty and Lagr, their performance and safety are highly sensitive to their hyperparameters. While Penalty with β=0.02𝛽0.02\beta=0.02italic_β = 0.02 and Lagr with λ0=1subscript𝜆01\lambda_{0}=1italic_λ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 1 generally have low costs, they also have frequent constraint violations. With β=0.5𝛽0.5\beta=0.5italic_β = 0.5 or λ0=5subscript𝜆05\lambda_{0}=5italic_λ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT 0 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT = 5, they prioritize safety but at the cost of high cumulative costs. Def-MARL, however, maintains a safety rate similar to the most conservative baselines (Penalty(0.5) and Lagr(5)) but has much lower costs. We point out that no single baseline method behaves considerably better on all the environments: the performance of the baseline methods varies wildly between environments, demonstrating the sensitivity of these algorithms to the choice of hyperparameters. On the contrary, Def-MARL, performs best in all environments, using a single set of constant hyperparameters, which demonstrates its insensitivity to the choice of hyperparameters.

(Q2): Def-MARL is able to reach the global optimum of the original problem. An important observation is that for Penalty and Lagr with a non-optimal λ𝜆\lambdaitalic_λ, the cost function optimized in their training process is different from the original cost function. Consequently, they can have different optimal solutions compared to the original problem. Therefore, even if their training converges, they may not reach the optimal solution to the original problem. In Fig. 4, the converged states of Def-MARL and four baselines are shown. Def-MARL reaches the original problem’s global optimum and covers all three goals. On the contrary, the optima of Penalty(0.02) and Lagr(1) are changed by the penalty term, so they choose to leave one agent behind to have a lower safety penalty. With an even more significant penalty, the optima of Penalty(0.5) and Lagr(5) are changed dramatically, and they forget the goal entirely and only focus on safety.

(Q3): Training of Def-MARL is more stable. To compare the training stability of Def-MARL and the Lagrangian method Lagr(lr), we plot their cost and safety rate during training in Fig. 5. Def-MARL has a smoother curve compared to Lagr(lr), supporting our theoretical analysis in Section III-B. Due to space limits, the plots for other environments and other baseline methods are provided in Section E-D.

(Q4): Def-MARL can scale to more agents while maintaining high performance and safety, but is limited by GPU memory due to centralized training. We test the limits of Def-MARL on the number of agents during training by comparing all methods on N=5,7𝑁57N=5,7italic_N = 5 , 7 with Formation and Line, and N=8,12,16𝑁81216N=8,12,16italic_N = 8 , 12 , 16 with Target (Fig. 6). We were unable to increase N𝑁Nitalic_N further due to GPU memory limitations due to the use of centralized training. For this experiment, we omit Lagr(lr) as it has the worst performance in MPE with N=3𝑁3N=3italic_N = 3. Def-MARL is closest to the upper left corner in all environments, and its performance does not decrease with an increasing number of agents.

(Q5): The trained policy from Def-MARL generalizes to much larger MAS. To test the generalization capabilities of Def-MARL, we test a policy trained with N=8𝑁8N=8italic_N = 8 on much larger MASs with up to N=512𝑁512N=512italic_N = 512 on Target (Table I) with the same agent density to avoid distribution shift. Def-MARL maintains a high safety rate and low costs despite being applied on a MAS with 64646464 times more agents.

V-C Ablation studies

Here we do ablation studies on the communication of zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT, and study the hyperparameter sensitivity of Def-MARL.

Is communicating zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT necessary? As introduced in Section IV-C, theoretically, all connected agents should communicate and reach a consensus on z=maxi⁡zi𝑧subscript𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖z=\max_{i}z_{i}italic_z = roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT. However, we observe in Section V-B that the agents can perform well even if agents take z←zi←𝑧subscript𝑧𝑖z\leftarrow z_{i}italic_z ← italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT without communicating to compute the maximum. We perform experiments on MPE (N=3𝑁3N=3italic_N = 3) to understand the impact of this approximation in Table III and see that using the approximation does not result in much performance difference compared to communicating zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT and using the maximum.

Varying ξ𝜉\xiitalic_ξ in the outer problem. To robustify our approach against estimation errors in Vhsuperscript𝑉ℎV^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT, we solve for a zisubscript𝑧𝑖z_{i}italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT that is slightly more conservative by modifying (15b) to Vψh⁢(oi,zi)≤−ξsuperscriptsubscript𝑉𝜓ℎsubscript𝑜𝑖subscript𝑧𝑖𝜉V_{\psi}^{h}(o_{i},z_{i})\leq-\xiitalic_V start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_ψ end_POSTSUBSCRIPT start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ( italic_o start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_i end_POSTSUBSCRIPT ) ≤ - italic_ξ (Section IV-C). We now perform experiments to study the effect of different choices of ξ𝜉\xiitalic_ξ (Table III) on Line (N=3𝑁3N=3italic_N = 3). The results show that higher values of ξ𝜉\xiitalic_ξ result in higher safety rates and slightly higher costs, while the reverse is true for smaller ξ𝜉\xiitalic_ξ. This matches our intuition that modifying (15b) can help improve constraint satisfaction when the learned Vhsuperscript𝑉ℎV^{h}italic_V start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_h end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT has estimation errors. We thus recommend choosing ξ𝜉\xiitalic_ξ close to ν𝜈\nuitalic_ν. We also provide the sensitivity analysis on more hyperparameters in Appendix E.

VI Hardware experiments

Finally, we conduct hardware experiments on a swarm of Crazyflie (CF) drones [27] to demonstrate Def-MARL’s ability to safely coordinate agents to complete complex collaborative tasks in the real world. We consider the following two tasks.

  • •

    Corridor. A swarm of drones collaboratively gets through a narrow corridor and reaches a set of goals without explicitly assigning drones to goals.

  • •

    Inspect. Two drones collaborate to maintain direct visual contact with a target drone that follows a path shaped like an eight while staying out of the target drone’s avoid zone. Visual contact only occurs when the line of sight to the target drone is not blocked by obstacles.

For both tasks, all drones have collision constraints with other drones and static obstacles. We visualize the tasks in Fig. 7.

Baselines. Def-MARL has demonstrated superior performance among RL methods in simulation experiments. Consequently, we do not consider RL methods as baselines for the hardware experiments. Instead, we present a comparative analysis between Def-MARL and model predictive control (MPC), a widely employed technique in practical robotic applications. Notably, MPC necessitates model dynamics knowledge, whereas Def-MARL does not. We compare Def-MARL against the following two MPC baselines.

  • •

    Decentralized. We consider a decentralized MPC method (DMPC), where each drone tries to individually minimize the total cost and prevents collision with other controlled drones by assuming a constant velocity motion model using the current measurement of their velocity.

  • •

    Centralized. We also test against a centralized MPC (CMPC) method to better disentangle phenomena related to numerical nonlinear optimization and performing decentralized control. This method uses the same cost function used by Def-MARL.

Both MPC methods are implemented in CasADi [4] with the SNOPT nonlinear optimizer [28]. Details for the hardware setup and experiment videos are provided in Appendix F.

VI-A Corridor

We run each algorithm from 16161616 random initial conditions and use the task success rate to measure their performance. The task is defined to be successful if all the goals are covered by the agents and all agents stay safe during the whole task. Accordingly, the success rate is defined as the number of successful tasks divided by 16161616. In our tests, the success rates of Def-MARL, CMPC, and DMPC are 100%percent100100\%100 %, 0%percent00\%0 %, and 62.5%percent62.562.5\%62.5 %. To analyze this result, we visualize the trajectory of Def-MARL and some failure cases of the baselines in Fig. 8.

CMPC is prone to local minima. We first compare Def-MARL with CMPC. Since we sum the distance from each goal to the closest drone, the cost function in this task is very nonconvex. Consequently, CMPC results in very suboptimal solutions, where only the closest drone to the goals attempts to reach the goals, with the remaining drones left on the other side. To alleviate this issue, although the original task does not have an explicit goal allocation, we provide a handicap to the CMPC methods and rerun the experiments with explicitly assigned goals for each drone. This simplifies the optimization problem by removing the discrete nature of goal assignment. We name this baseline CMPCwA (CMPC with assignment). However, even with explicit goal assignments, we still see that sometimes one of the drones in the team gets stuck behind the corridor, resulting in a success rate of 87.5%percent87.587.5\%87.5 %. In contrast, Def-MARL does not succumb to this local minimum and completes the task with a success rate of 100%percent100100\%100 %.

DMPC has unsafe cases. As DMPC without goal assignment will also suffer from similar issues as CMPC, we choose to assign goals for each agent in this baseline. This results in a simpler problem, as explained above. However, unlike CMPCwA, the agents using DMPC do not know the actual actions of the other agents and can only make predictions based on their observations because of the decentralized nature of DMPC. Therefore, collisions may occur if the other agents behave significantly differently from the predictions. In the DMPC row of Fig. 8, the agents collide666For our safety, the safety radius of the drones is larger than their actual radius. Here, we mean they have entered each others’ safety radius, although they have not collided in reality. in the middle of the tasks, causing the MPC optimization problem to become infeasible and preventing the agents from reaching their goals.777Some MPC-based methods can solve the Corridor environment [43, 68] but assume pre-assigned goals. Additionally, these approaches need additional methods for collision avoidance (e.g., Buffered Voronoi Cells [91], on-demand collision avoidance approaches [42]), which require more domain knowledge.

Def-MARL can scale up to 7777 agents. We also test the scalability of Def-MARL with 7777 CF drones in the same environment. Notably, the size of the environment remains unchanged, so the environment becomes much more crowded and thus more challenging. We test Def-MARL with 9999 different random initial conditions, and it maintains a success rate of 100%percent100100\%100 %. We visualize one of the trajectories in Fig. 9. Note that we were limited to only 7777 drones here due to only having 7777 drones available. However, given the simulation results, we are hopeful that Def-MARL can scale to larger hardware swarms.

VI-B Inspect

We also run each algorithm from 16161616 different random initial conditions. Note that the agents may not be able to observe the target at their initial positions at the first step. In this environment, the team of two drones should maximize the duration where the goal is observed by at least one drone. Measuring the task performance by the number of timesteps where the target is not visible, we obtain that the performance of Def-MARL, CMPC, and DMPC are 85.5±42.9plus-or-minus85.542.985.5\pm 42.985.5 ± 42.9, 206±53.2plus-or-minus20653.2206\pm 53.2206 ± 53.2, and 251±59.1plus-or-minus25159.1251\pm 59.1251 ± 59.1, respectively. We also report the safety rate, defined as the ratio of tasks where all agents stay safe, which are 100%percent100100\%100 %, 100%percent100100\%100 %, and 43.75%percent43.7543.75\%43.75 %, respectively. We visualize the trajectories of different methods in Fig. 10.

Agents using Def-MARL have better collaboration. The Inspect is designed such that collaboration between the agents is necessary to observe the target without any downtime. One agent cannot observe the target all the time on its own because the agent’s observation can be blocked by obstacles. It also cannot simply follow the target because of the avoid region. Using Def-MARL, the agents learn collaborative behaviors such as waiting on each side of the obstacles and taking turns to observe the target when the target is on their side. The MPC methods, however, do not have such global optimal behavior but get stuck in local minima. For example, CMPC only moves the closest drone to the target, leaving the other drone stationary, while DMPC makes both agents chase after the target without collaboration. Therefore, both MPC methods have small periods of time where neither drone has visual contact with the target. In addition, similar to in Corridor, we observe that DMPC sometimes results in collisions due to the lack of coordination between drones.

VII Conclusion

To construct safe distributed policies for real-world multi-agent systems, this paper introduces Def-MARL for the multi-agent safe optimal control problem, which defines safety as zero constraint violation. Def-MARL takes advantage of the epigraph form of the original problem to address the training instability of Lagrangian methods in the zero-constraint violation setting. We provide a theoretical result showing that the centralized epigraph form can be solved in a distributed fashion by each agent, which enables distributed execution of Def-MARL. Simulation results on MPE and the Safe Multi-agent MuJoCo environments suggest that, unlike baseline methods, Def-MARL uses a constant set of hyperparameters across all environments, and achieves a safety rate similar to the most conservative baseline and similar performance to the baselines that prioritize performance but violate safety constraints. Hardware results on the Crazyflie drones demonstrate Def-MARL’s ability to solve complex collaborative tasks safely in the real world.

VIII Limitations

The theoretical analysis in Section IV-C suggests that the connected agents must communicate z𝑧zitalic_z and reach a consensus. If the communication on z𝑧zitalic_z is disabled, although our experiments show that the agents still perform similarly, the theoretical optimality guarantee may not be valid. In addition, the framework does not consider noise, disturbances in the dynamics, or communication delays between agents. Finally, as a safe RL method, although safety can be theoretically guaranteed under the optimal value function and policy, this does not hold under inexact minimization of the losses. We leave tackling these issues as future work.

In addition, Zhang, So, and Fan are supported by the MIT-DSTA program.   • Yang et al. [2020] Yaodong Yang, Jianye Hao, Ben Liao, Kun Shao, Guangyong Chen, Wulong Liu, and Hongyao Tang. Qatten: A general framework for cooperative multiagent reinforcement learning. arXiv preprint arXiv:2002.03939, 2020.
  • Yu et al. [2022a] Chao Yu, Akash Velu, Eugene Vinitsky, Jiaxuan Gao, Yu Wang, Alexandre Bayen, and Yi Wu. The surprising effectiveness of ppo in cooperative multi-agent games. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 35:24611–24624, 2022a.
  • Yu et al. [2022b] Dongjie Yu, Haitong Ma, Shengbo Li, and Jianyu Chen. Reachability constrained reinforcement learning. In International conference on machine learning, pages 25636–25655. PMLR, 2022b.
  • Zanon and Gros [2020] Mario Zanon and Sébastien Gros. Safe reinforcement learning using robust mpc. IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control, 66(8):3638–3652, 2020.
  • Zhang et al. [2018] Kaiqing Zhang, Zhuoran Yang, Han Liu, Tong Zhang, and Tamer Basar. Fully decentralized multi-agent reinforcement learning with networked agents. In International conference on machine learning, pages 5872–5881. PMLR, 2018.
  • Zhang et al. [2021] Kaiqing Zhang, Zhuoran Yang, and Tamer Başar. Multi-agent reinforcement learning: A selective overview of theories and algorithms. Handbook of reinforcement learning and control, pages 321–384, 2021.
  • Zhang et al. [2023] Songyuan Zhang, Kunal Garg, and Chuchu Fan. Neural graph control barrier functions guided distributed collision-avoidance multi-agent control. In Conference on Robot Learning, pages 2373–2392. PMLR, 2023.
  • Zhang et al. [2025a] Songyuan Zhang, Oswin So, Mitchell Black, and Chuchu Fan. Discrete GCBF proximal policy optimization for multi-agent safe optimal control. In The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations, 2025a.
  • Zhang et al. [2025b] Songyuan Zhang, Oswin So, Kunal Garg, and Chuchu Fan. GCBF+: A neural graph control barrier function framework for distributed safe multiagent control. IEEE Transactions on Robotics, 41:1533–1552, 2025b.
  • Zhang et al. [2019] Wenbo Zhang, Osbert Bastani, and Vijay Kumar. Mamps: Safe multi-agent reinforcement learning via model predictive shielding. arXiv preprint arXiv:1910.12639, 2019.
  • Zhao et al. [2021] Weiye Zhao, Tairan He, and Changliu Liu. Model-free safe control for zero-violation reinforcement learning. In 5th Annual Conference on Robot Learning, 2021.
  • Zhao et al. [2024] Youpeng Zhao, Yaodong Yang, Zhenbo Lu, Wengang Zhou, and Houqiang Li. Multi-agent first order constrained optimization in policy space. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 36, 2024.
  • Zhou et al. [2017] Dingjiang Zhou, Zijian Wang, Saptarshi Bandyopadhyay, and Mac Schwager. Fast, on-line collision avoidance for dynamic vehicles using buffered voronoi cells. IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters, 2(2):1047–1054, 2017.
  • Zhu et al. [2020] Edward L Zhu, Yvonne R Stürz, Ugo Rosolia, and Francesco Borrelli. Trajectory optimization for nonlinear multi-agent systems using decentralized learning model predictive control. In 2020 59th IEEE Conference on Decision and Control (CDC), pages 6198–6203. IEEE, 2020.

Appendix A Proof of Proposition 1

{mdframed}

[style=ThmFrame]

Proof:

Under the dynamics xk+1=f⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))superscript𝑥𝑘1𝑓superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘x^{k+1}=f(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT = italic_f ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ), we have

V⁢(xk,zk;π)𝑉superscript𝑥𝑘superscript𝑧𝑘𝜋\displaystyle V(x^{k},z^{k};\pi)italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) =max⁡{maxp≥k⁡h⁢(xp),∑p≥kl⁢(xp,π⁢(xp))−zk}absentsubscript𝑝𝑘ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑝subscript𝑝𝑘𝑙superscript𝑥𝑝𝜋superscript𝑥𝑝superscript𝑧𝑘\displaystyle=\max\left\{\max_{p\geq k}h(x^{p}),\sum_{p\geq k}l(x^{p},\pi(x^{p% }))-z^{k}\right\}= roman_max { roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) - italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT } (16)
=max⁡{max⁡{h⁢(xk),maxp≥k+1⁡h⁢(xp)},∑p≥k+1l⁢(xp,π⁢(xp))+l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))−zk}absentℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘subscript𝑝𝑘1ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑝subscript𝑝𝑘1𝑙superscript𝑥𝑝𝜋superscript𝑥𝑝𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘superscript𝑧𝑘\displaystyle=\max\left\{\max\{h(x^{k}),\max_{p\geq k+1}h(x^{p})\},\sum_{p\geq k% +1}l(x^{p},\pi(x^{p}))+l(x^{k},\pi(x^{k}))-z^{k}\right\}= roman_max { roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) } , ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) + italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) - italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT }
=max⁡{max⁡{h⁢(xk),maxp≥k+1⁡h⁢(xp)},∑p≥k+1l⁢(xp,π⁢(xp))−[zk−l⁢(xk,π⁢(xk))]⏟≔zk+1}absentℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘subscript𝑝𝑘1ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑝subscript𝑝𝑘1𝑙superscript𝑥𝑝𝜋superscript𝑥𝑝subscript⏟delimited-[]superscript𝑧𝑘𝑙superscript𝑥𝑘𝜋superscript𝑥𝑘≔absentsuperscript𝑧𝑘1\displaystyle=\max\left\{\max\{h(x^{k}),\max_{p\geq k+1}h(x^{p})\},\sum_{p\geq k% +1}l(x^{p},\pi(x^{p}))-{\underbrace{\color[rgb]{0,0,0}\Big{[}z^{k}-l(x^{k},\pi% (x^{k}))\Big{]}}_{\coloneqq z^{k+1}}}\right\}= roman_max { roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) } , ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) - under⏟ start_ARG [ italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT - italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) ] end_ARG start_POSTSUBSCRIPT ≔ italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT end_POSTSUBSCRIPT }
=max⁡{h⁢(xk),max⁡{maxp≥k+1⁡h⁢(xp),∑p≥k+1l⁢(xp,π⁢(xp))−zk+1}}absentℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘subscript𝑝𝑘1ℎsuperscript𝑥𝑝subscript𝑝𝑘1𝑙superscript𝑥𝑝𝜋superscript𝑥𝑝superscript𝑧𝑘1\displaystyle=\max\left\{h(x^{k}),\max\left\{\max_{p\geq k+1}h(x^{p}),\sum_{p% \geq k+1}l(x^{p},\pi(x^{p}))-z^{k+1}\right\}\right\}= roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , roman_max { roman_max start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , ∑ start_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_p ≥ italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUBSCRIPT italic_l ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_π ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_p end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) ) - italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT } }
=max⁡{h⁢(xk),V⁢(xk+1,zk+1;π)},absentℎsuperscript𝑥𝑘𝑉superscript𝑥𝑘1superscript𝑧𝑘1𝜋\displaystyle=\max\left\{h(x^{k}),V(x^{k+1},z^{k+1};\pi)\right\},= roman_max { italic_h ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ) , italic_V ( italic_x start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT , italic_z start_POSTSUPERSCRIPT italic_k + 1 end_POSTSUPERSCRIPT ; italic_π ) } ,
